findings.title: Package @proj-airi/desktop-shell has no consumer in monorepo
findings.description: The new @proj-airi/desktop-shell package exports getActiveWindow, ClipboardMonitor, and ShortcutManager, but no other package or app in the monorepo imports or depends on it. A grep for '@proj-airi/desktop-shell' and 'desktop-shell' across all .ts, .json, and .vue files returns only self-references (its own package.json, vitest.config.ts entry, and tasks.json). This is orphan code -- dead-on-arrival with no live entry point in any application.
findings.suggestion: Wire the package into at least one consumer (e.g., apps/stage-tamagotchi Electron main process services) before committing. Add it as a dependency in the consumer's package.json and import/instantiate its exports from a service that runs at app startup. The integration-rules require every new module to trace to at least one live entry point.

findings.title: No structured logging in desktop-shell package
findings.code_snippet: reject(new Error('Failed to get active window', { cause: error }))
findings.description: The entire desktop-shell package (active-window.ts, clipboard-monitor.ts, global-shortcuts.ts) contains zero logging. Errors in getActiveWindow are rejected but never logged. ClipboardMonitor and ShortcutManager delegate errors to an optional onError callback but produce no structured log output. The project standard requires @guiiai/logg (useLogg pattern) for all new packages. Without logging, failures in window detection, clipboard polling, or shortcut registration will be invisible in production.
findings.suggestion: Add @guiiai/logg as a dependency and create a logger via useLogg('desktop-shell') or per-module loggers (useLogg('desktop-shell:active-window'), etc.). Log at INFO level on start/stop lifecycle events, WARN on recoverable errors (shortcut registration failure, clipboard read failure), and ERROR on fatal failures (getActiveWindow rejection).

findings.title: ShortcutManager.registered set true even when registrations fail
findings.description: After iterating over all bindings, line 49 unconditionally sets this.registered = true regardless of how many registrations actually succeeded. If all registrations fail (returning false or throwing), the manager still reports isRegistered === true and subsequent registerAll() calls become no-ops. This means a consumer cannot retry registration after transient failures. Additionally, unregisterAll() will call the underlying unregisterAllFn even though nothing was successfully registered.
findings.suggestion: Track whether at least one binding registered successfully. Either: (a) set this.registered = true only if successCount > 0, or (b) track per-binding registration state so unregisterAll can skip unneeded cleanup and registerAll can retry failed bindings. A simple fix: add a successCount variable, increment on success, and set this.registered = successCount > 0.

findings.title: getActiveWindow hardcodes macOS AppleScript with no platform guard
findings.code_snippet:
const APPLESCRIPT = `
tell application "System Events"
  set frontApp to first application process whose frontmost is true
  ...
end tell
`
findings.description: The getActiveWindow function uses osascript (AppleScript) which only works on macOS. The function has no runtime platform check and will fail with a cryptic error on Windows/Linux. The package description says 'macOS desktop integration' but the exported API gives no indication of platform restriction. The integration test guards with describe.runIf(platform() === 'darwin') but the production code has no such guard. The package lists std-env as a dependency (presumably for platform detection) but never imports it.
findings.suggestion: Add an explicit platform check at the top of getActiveWindow() that rejects with a clear error message on non-macOS platforms: if (process.platform !== 'darwin') reject(new Error('getActiveWindow is only supported on macOS')). Alternatively, if std-env was intended for this purpose, import and use its isMacOS check. Either way, document the platform restriction in the JSDoc and README.

findings.title: std-env declared as dependency but never imported
findings.description: The package.json declares std-env as a runtime dependency, but no source file under packages/desktop-shell/src/ imports from 'std-env'. A grep across the entire src directory confirms zero references. This is dead weight in the dependency tree and suggests an incomplete implementation where platform detection was planned but not wired up.
findings.suggestion: Either remove std-env from dependencies (if platform detection is not needed), or import and use it for the platform guard in active-window.ts (e.g., import { isMacOS } from 'std-env'). Do not ship unused dependencies.

findings.title: ShortcutManagerOptions.unregister is declared but never used
findings.description: The ShortcutManagerOptions interface requires an 'unregister' function (line 30) for removing individual shortcut registrations. However, ShortcutManager never references or calls this function -- it only stores and calls unregisterAll (the bulk variant). Consumers must provide this function to satisfy the type contract but it is silently ignored. This is a dead interface member that creates confusion about expected behavior.
findings.suggestion: Either (a) remove 'unregister' from ShortcutManagerOptions if per-shortcut unregistration is not needed, or (b) implement per-binding unregistration in ShortcutManager (e.g., for re-registering a single changed binding). Option (a) is simpler and avoids ISP violation.

findings.title: ClipboardMonitor.lastText is ephemeral in-memory state
findings.code_snippet:
private lastText: string
private timer: ReturnType<typeof setInterval> | null = null
...
this.lastText = ''
findings.description: ClipboardMonitor tracks lastText (the previously seen clipboard content) as an in-memory private field initialized to empty string. On service restart, this will be reset and the first poll will trigger a spurious onChange event for whatever is currently in the clipboard (since it will differ from the empty initial value). For a desktop app that restarts, this means a false clipboard-change notification on every launch.
findings.suggestion: Accept an optional initialText parameter in ClipboardMonitorOptions (defaulting to undefined/null) and, when provided, use it to seed lastText. Alternatively, in the start() method, read the clipboard once to initialize lastText before starting the interval timer (which it already does on line 26). The current code at line 26 does handle this correctly for the first start -- but if stop() is called and start() is called again, the stale lastText from the previous run is kept. Consider whether re-start should re-read the clipboard to avoid false positives.

findings.title: DEFAULT_BINDINGS hardcodes shortcut accelerators
findings.code_snippet:
export const DEFAULT_BINDINGS = [
  { accelerator: 'CommandOrControl+Shift+A', action: 'toggle-panel' as ShortcutAction },
  { accelerator: 'CommandOrControl+Shift+V', action: 'voice-input' as ShortcutAction },
  ...
] as const
findings.description: Shortcut key accelerators are hardcoded as compile-time constants. Users who need to customize shortcuts (accessibility, keyboard layout conflicts) have no mechanism to override them without code changes. While ShortcutManager accepts bindings as a constructor option, the DEFAULT_BINDINGS constant encourages consumers to pass it directly, baking in the specific key combinations.
findings.suggestion: Make DEFAULT_BINDINGS a fallback that can be overridden via user configuration. The consumer (e.g., Electron main process) should load shortcut bindings from a persisted configuration store (user preferences) and fall back to DEFAULT_BINDINGS only when no custom config exists. This is a design consideration for the integration layer rather than a change to this package, but document the intent clearly.

positive_observations: Clean Functional Core / Imperative Shell separation: ClipboardMonitor and ShortcutManager accept IO callbacks via constructor options, keeping core logic pure and testable without mocks
Excellent test coverage with thorough edge cases: empty input, unicode, double-start idempotency, error propagation with and without onError handlers
Proper error wrapping with cause chains: new Error('...', { cause: err }) provides debuggable error context throughout
getActiveWindow uses execFile with timeout (5000ms) rather than exec, avoiding shell injection and preventing hangs
Test for ClipboardMonitor uses vi.useFakeTimers for deterministic timer testing -- clean and reliable approach
Integration test for active-window guards with describe.runIf(platform() === 'darwin') and includes a sensible osascript availability check
